title: Search in a Sorted 2D Grid

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: n x n grid where each row is sorted left-to-right and each column is sorted top-to-bottom. Find target x.
rules: If A[i][j] < x: x can't be in row i (all to the left are <= A[i][j]); move down (i++).
If A[i][j] > x: x can't be in column j (all below are >= A[i][j]); move left (j--).
setup.question: What is the time complexity?

learningObjectives: Choose the right starting corner (top-right or bottom-left).
Argue that each step eliminates a row or a column.
Justify the O(n) (more precisely, O(rows + cols)) bound.

stages.label: Why top-right works
stages.prompt: Why must we start at the TOP-RIGHT (or BOTTOM-LEFT) corner, not the top-left?

options.text: Top-right element is the row-max AND column-min. Comparing with x tells us in which direction to prune. Top-LEFT is row-min and column-min -- the comparison doesn't disambiguate.
Top-left also works -- it's the same by symmetry.

stages.explanation: At the top-right corner: if A[1][n] < x, x can't be in row 1; if A[1][n] > x, x can't be in column n. Each comparison kills an entire row OR column. Top-left would say 'x might be ANYWHERE if A[1][1] < x', useless.

stages.label: Commit -- complexity
stages.prompt: How many steps in the worst case?

options.text: At most 2n: each step eliminates at least one row or column, and there are only n of each. So O(n).
O(log n) -- it's like binary search.

stages.explanation: We start at (1, n) and end at (n+1, 0) in the worst case. Each step changes i by +1 or j by -1. Total steps <= 2n -1. NOT log n -- a naive intuition fails because the grid is only PARTIALLY sorted (rows and cols, not the whole thing).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Could you do better than O(n) with a smarter strategy? (Yes -- via two-stage diagonal binary search you can get O(n log(n/m)) for an n x m grid, but it's intricate.)
What if rows are sorted but columns are not? (You'd binary-search each row -- O(n log n).)
Apply this to financial heat-maps: e.g., a price matrix where rows = strikes (sorted), columns = maturities (sorted). The top-right pointer is a fast way to locate a target implied vol.
